items.q: What does Frank's dependency theory argue?
items.correct: That LICs (periphery) are kept poor by exploitative trade relationships with HICs (core) — underdevelopment is actively produced by capitalism, not a stage that countries grow out of.
items.misconception: Dependency theory says LICs are poor because they depend too much on foreign aid.
items.random_wrong: Dependency theory is the same as Rostow's modernisation theory.
items.why: Dependency theory is structural and critical, opposing Rostow. The 'dependency' is on the global economic system, not aid. Students confuse the word 'dependency' with aid reliance, missing the political-economic critique.

items.q: Why does deforestation increase flood risk?
items.correct: Trees intercept rainfall, their roots increase infiltration and transpiration removes water — removing them increases surface runoff and reduces lag time, peaking river levels faster.
items.misconception: Deforestation causes floods because trees normally hold water in their branches forever.
items.random_wrong: Deforestation has no effect on rivers — only rainfall matters.
items.why: Interception is temporary but it delays and reduces runoff. Without forests, water reaches rivers faster and in greater volume — interacting with antecedent soil moisture to drive flood peaks.

items.q: What is the main social impact of gentrification?
items.correct: Displacement of long-term lower-income residents as rents and property prices rise, often along with loss of community businesses and cultural identity — even if the area looks 'improved'.
items.misconception: Gentrification only has positive effects because it makes areas nicer.
items.random_wrong: Gentrification means evicting all residents by force.
items.why: Improvements aren't free — they're paid for by displacement. Students focus on visible upgrades and miss the demographic shift. Many evaluation questions ask 'for whom is gentrification beneficial?'.

items.q: Which African region suffers severe desertification?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Sahel. “Maghreb” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What is the maximum rainfall in hot deserts?
items.why: It's 250mm. “100mm” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What percentage of Northern Hemisphere is permafrost?
items.why: The real figure is 25 %. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
